content.text: The Model Context Protocol is language-agnostic: any language with an MCP SDK can host a server or client. All SDKs implement the same JSON-RPC 2.0 wire protocol, so a TypeScript server works with a Python client without modification. This section covers the Python and TypeScript SDKs in depth — the two most widely used — and maps the shared concepts that let you transfer knowledge between them.

attrs.alt: MCP SDK landscape: Python, TypeScript, and other language SDKs all converge on the same JSON-RPC 2.0 wire protocol. Each SDK provides transport framing, a method dispatcher, type-safe request/response objects, and lifecycle management.

content.text: The protocol is the contract. Once you understand the JSON-RPC methods — initialize, tools/list, tools/call, resources/read, prompts/get — every SDK is just idiomatic plumbing for those same messages.
) is asyncio-native and ships both the server framework and the client library. It is the reference for examples throughout this course.
Client; list/call tools, read resources, get prompts
Config for launching a server subprocess
Content block types returned by tools and resources

import asyncio
import mcp.server.stdio
from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.types import Tool, TextContent, CallToolResult

app = Server("my-server")

@app.list_tools()
async def list_tools():
    return [Tool(name="ping", description="Return pong", inputSchema={"type":"object","properties":{}})]

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "ping":
        return [TextContent(type="text", text="pong")]
    return CallToolResult(content=[TextContent(type="text", text="Unknown tool")], isError=True)

async def main():
    async with mcp.server.stdio.stdio_server() as (r, w):
        await app.run(r, w, app.create_initialization_options())

asyncio.run(main())

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def main():
    params = StdioServerParameters(command="python", args=["server.py"])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool("ping", {})
            print(result.content[0].text)   # "pong"

asyncio.run(main())
) is the reference implementation — it tracks the protocol specification most closely. It uses Zod for schema validation and ships both
The concepts are identical; the idioms differ. This table maps the key terms:
Because all SDKs implement the same JSON-RPC 2.0 wire protocol, mixing languages works transparently. A Python client can call a TypeScript server, and a Go server can be driven by a Python agent.

# Python client connecting to a TypeScript server (node server.js)
import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def main():
    params = StdioServerParameters(
        command="node",
        args=["server.js"],   # TypeScript server, compiled to JS
    )
    async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            print([t.name for t in tools.tools])  # works identically

asyncio.run(main())
The Python client sends the same JSON-RPC
message it would send to any server. The TypeScript server responds with the standard schema. Neither side knows nor cares what language the other uses.
— best when your tools call Python data libraries (pandas, SQLAlchemy, ML models) or when you want the most tutorial coverage.
— best when your tools are already Node.js services, when you want Zod's compile-time type safety on tool arguments, or when you are building a browser-side MCP host.
— best when you need a tiny binary with minimal startup time, suitable for embedding in CLI tools or containers where Python startup overhead matters.
— best when your tools wrap JVM-based enterprise services (Spring Boot APIs, JDBC databases, Kafka consumers).
Beyond the core tool handlers, the Python SDK exposes notifications, progress updates, logging hooks, and explicit capability declarations.
Long-running tools can push progress events to the client while the operation is underway. The client must opt in by passing a

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.types import Tool, TextContent
from mcp.server.models import InitializationOptions
import asyncio

app = Server("bulk-processor")

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "process_items":
        items = arguments["items"]
        progress_token = arguments.get("_meta", {}).get("progressToken")

        results = []
        for i, item in enumerate(items):
            # Simulate work
            await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
            results.append(f"processed:{item}")

            # Notify client of progress
            if progress_token:
                await app.request_context.session.send_progress_notification(
                    progress_token=progress_token,
                    progress=i + 1,
                    total=len(items),
                )

        return [TextContent(type="text", text="\n".join(results))]
) lets a server send structured log entries to the host for display. Use this instead of printing to stderr when you want logs visible in the client UI.

from mcp.types import LoggingLevel

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "sync_data":
        await app.request_context.session.send_log_message(
            level=LoggingLevel.info,
            data="Starting data sync",
            logger="sync",
        )
        # ... do work ...
        await app.request_context.session.send_log_message(
            level=LoggingLevel.info,
            data="Sync complete: 142 records updated",
            logger="sync",
        )
        return [TextContent(type="text", text="done")]

By default the SDK infers capabilities from registered handlers. You can override this to advertise optional features (resource subscriptions, experimental flags) that clients check before calling advanced endpoints.

from mcp.types import ServerCapabilities, ToolsCapability, ResourcesCapability
from mcp.server.models import InitializationOptions

# Passed to app.run()
init_options = InitializationOptions(
    server_name="my-server",
    server_version="2.0.0",
    capabilities=ServerCapabilities(
        tools=ToolsCapability(listChanged=True),    # server can notify when tool list changes
        resources=ResourcesCapability(subscribe=False, listChanged=False),
    ),
)

async def main():
    async with mcp.server.stdio.stdio_server() as (r, w):
        await app.run(r, w, init_options)
The TypeScript SDK supports returning multiple content blocks from a single tool call. Use this to return both structured data and a human-readable summary.
The TypeScript SDK supports resource templates — URI patterns with variable segments. This allows a client to discover the URI structure without enumerating every possible resource.
Because the wire protocol is standard JSON-RPC 2.0, you can write cross-language integration tests: a Python test suite can test a TypeScript server by spawning it with

# test_ts_server.py — Python client testing a TypeScript server
import sys, pytest
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

TS_SERVER = StdioServerParameters(command="node", args=["dist/server.js"])

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_ts_server_tools():
    async with stdio_client(TS_SERVER) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            names = {t.name for t in tools.tools}
            assert "get_weather" in names

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_ts_server_add():
    async with stdio_client(TS_SERVER) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool("add", {"a": 7, "b": 8})
            assert result.content[0].text == "15"
This approach also validates that your server conforms to the protocol correctly — any deviation from the spec will cause the Python SDK's strict JSON-RPC parser to reject the response.

# pyproject.toml
[project]
name = "my-mcp-server"
version = "0.1.0"
dependencies = ["mcp>=1.0", "httpx"]

[project.scripts]
my-mcp-server = "my_mcp_server.__main__:main"

# src/my_mcp_server/__main__.py
import asyncio
from .server import main
asyncio.run(main())
After publishing to npm or PyPI, users install once and configure their host via
All MCP SDKs implement the same JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol; servers and clients in different languages interoperate without adaptation.

Swap transport classes (stdio ↔ SSE) to change deployment topology; the capability API stays the same.
Choose your SDK based on where your tool logic already lives — the protocol is never the bottleneck.
